skill.name: onvifscan
skill.description: Guides authorized ONVIF device security checks, including authentication testing and controlled credential audit workflows for IP cameras.
skill.summary: Assess authorized ONVIF cameras for exposed endpoints and weak credentials.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static backtick, hardcoded IP, hardcoded URL, and weak-cryptography findings are mostly false positives from Markdown examples and prose. However, the skill explicitly instructs network scanning, credential brute forcing, and use of an option that may test destructive endpoints. This is a high-risk dual-use security skill and should not be published without authorization safeguards and abuse controls.

high_findings.title: Credential Brute Forcing Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill describes credential brute-forcing attacks and provides command examples for running them against ONVIF devices. This can enable unauthorized access attempts against IP cameras if used outside an approved assessment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly names brute-forcing attacks and includes concrete brute command usage. The behavior is dual-use, but the offensive capability is explicit.

high_findings.title: Potentially Destructive Endpoint Testing
high_findings.description: The skill documents an all-endpoints option and warns that it may test destructive endpoints. Running this against live cameras could alter state or disrupt device operation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file itself states that the all-endpoints option includes potentially destructive tests. The risk depends on target authorization and device behavior.

high_findings.title: Network Scanning Without Authorization Controls
high_findings.description: The skill asks for a device URL or IP and then directs execution of scan commands. It does not require proof of authorization, define scope controls, or require confirmation before testing external targets.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network scanning workflow is clear, and no authorization gate appears in the scanned file. Legitimate defensive use is possible, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Target Passed To External Command
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant to collect a user-provided device URL or IP and place it into an onvifscan command. If the command is assembled unsafely by a host tool, malformed input could create command execution or scanning scope risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill does not itself execute code, but it instructs command construction with user input. The impact depends on the host execution environment.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings appear to be Markdown inline code and fenced examples. No Ruby execution or embedded script file is present in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is Markdown documentation, not executable Ruby source. The commands are examples, although they still describe risky security actions.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Examples Are Documentation Samples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and IP addresses are private-network examples used in command documentation. They are not evidence of exfiltration or a fixed external callback destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses use 192.168.1.100 in examples, which is private address space. No outbound collection endpoint is shown.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings do not correspond to cryptographic implementation in the scanned file. The referenced lines contain descriptive text, option notes, or Markdown examples rather than crypto calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic API, hash call, or algorithm implementation appears at the cited locations. These are scanner keyword matches rather than confirmed crypto use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Brute Force Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented brute subcommand can automate credential guessing against protected ONVIF endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and option descriptions directly support credential guessing with wordlists.

dangerous_patterns.title: Potentially Destructive Scan Option
dangerous_patterns.description: The all-endpoints option may test destructive endpoints and should require explicit authorization and confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly warns that the option may involve destructive endpoint tests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unscoped Network Target Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow accepts arbitrary device URLs or IP addresses without documenting ownership verification or scope limits.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow asks for a target and proceeds to command execution, with no authorization checklist in the scanned file.

content.value_statement: Exposed ONVIF services can leave cameras open to unauthorized access or weak credential attacks. This skill helps authorized testers plan focused authentication checks and review results responsibly.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to run ONVIF authentication checks against authorized devices.
Shows command patterns for auth scans and verbose endpoint review.
Documents credential audit options using username and password wordlists.
Highlights output format choices for text, JSON, or quiet results.
Warns that all-endpoints testing may include destructive checks.
limitations: Does not include the onvifscan binary or validate that it is installed.
Does not prove that the user owns or is allowed to test a target.
Does not provide remediation steps for every camera vendor or firmware.
May be unsafe on production devices without strict scope and approval.

use_cases.title: Internal Camera Exposure Review
use_cases.description: Check whether approved cameras expose ONVIF endpoints without authentication.

use_cases.title: IoT Assessment Preparation
use_cases.description: Plan authorized ONVIF tests and choose safe output formats before field work.

use_cases.title: Weak Credential Audit
use_cases.description: Run limited credential checks with approved wordlists and documented scope.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Auth Check
prompt_templates.prompt: I have approval to test one ONVIF camera at <target>. Help me plan a non-destructive auth scan and list the checks to confirm first.

prompt_templates.title: Review Auth Scan Results
prompt_templates.prompt: I ran an authorized ONVIF auth scan and have the text output. Help me identify exposed endpoints and recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Credential Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to test approved ONVIF devices for weak credentials. Help me define scope, wordlist rules, rate limits, and reporting fields.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Assessment Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Using these authorized ONVIF scan findings, create a concise risk report with evidence, business impact, and remediation priorities.

output_examples.input: Plan a non-destructive auth scan for one approved camera.
output_examples.output: A short checklist covering authorization, target address, scan type, output format, and a reminder to avoid all-endpoints testing.

output_examples.input: Summarize ONVIF auth scan findings for operations staff.
output_examples.output: A plain-language summary of exposed endpoints, authentication status, affected device, and recommended hardening actions.

output_examples.input: Prepare a scoped credential audit plan.
output_examples.output: A testing plan with approved targets, approved wordlists, attempt limits, stop conditions, and documentation requirements.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and exact target scope before running any scan.
Start with non-destructive authentication checks before any credential audit.
Record device owner, target, time window, command options, and observed impact.
anti_patterns: Do not scan public IP addresses or third-party cameras without permission.
Do not use all-endpoints testing on production devices without explicit approval.
Do not run broad credential guessing with unapproved wordlists or no stop condition.

faq.question: What does this skill help test?
faq.answer: It helps plan ONVIF authentication checks and controlled credential audits for authorized IP camera assessments.

faq.question: Is this safe for production cameras?
faq.answer: Only limited auth checks may be appropriate. Destructive or brute-force options need strict approval and a maintenance window.

faq.question: Does the skill install onvifscan?
faq.answer: No. It documents how to use the onvifscan command when the tool is already available.

faq.question: Can it test devices I do not own?
faq.answer: No. Use it only on devices where you have explicit authorization to test.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated high?
faq.answer: The skill includes network scanning, credential brute forcing, and an option that may test destructive endpoints.

faq.question: What should I do with findings?
faq.answer: Disable unauthenticated access, change weak credentials, update firmware, restrict network access, and document remediation.
